text: The surgeon begins posterior dissection between the bladder and rectum by dividing the peritoneum. A dilated cystic structure is visible, replacing the prostate, with internal communication to the urethra and external communication to the perianal region.

During intraoperative cystoscopy, a large opening is observed at the seven o'clock position of the prostatic urethra. The surgeon negotiates the tract, revealing a large cystic sac. Anterior dissection is initiated through the space of Retzius due to incomplete dissection of the cystic sac via the posterior approach.

The surgeon approaches the cystic sac from the left side of the bladder, incising the endopelvic fascia and dissecting it from surrounding structures. The sac, replacing the prostate, is visualized communicating internally with the prostatic urethra and externally with the perianal region. Stay sutures are applied for better delineation, and the sac is divided. Simultaneous cystoscopy identifies the fistulous opening into the urethra, and the remnant sac is traced and resected using the stay sutures.

The surgeon uses electrocautery carefully to avoid injuring the neurovascular bundle while another team injects methylene blue through the external opening of the fistula. The methylene blue delineates the tract, allowing for its complete excision using a combined abdominal perineal approach.
The surgeon repairs the urethra using Vicryl 3-0 sutures. The defect in the pelvis is closed with StrataFix sutures, while the perineal wound is left open to heal by secondary intention.
